segments.s660-g49: همی is an adverb (poetic) meaning 'always/continually' that precedes the predicate. در پناه تو is a prepositional phrase 'in your protection' with the possessor تو after پناه (typical Persian order: noun + possessor). باید + verb (نشست) expresses necessity/obligation ('one should / it is necessary to sit/remain'); here the verb is non‑finite/indeterminate and the subject is general/impersonal.
segments.s660-g50: The clause repeats اگر to list two possible conditions. اگر زیردست است is a full conditional 'if he is a subordinate' (است = copula 'is'). The second shorter clause اگر در پرست omits an explicit copula (copula is implied) and names a second condition 'if (he is) a dar‑parast / court‑attendant'; the clauses are coordinate alternatives introduced by repeated اگر.
segments.s660-g51: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause here ('when' / 'if'). نیکی کند با تو is the subordinate clause 'does a good deed to you' (کند = 3rd person present 'does'). پاداش کن is the main imperative 'reward (him/it)' — the object is understood from the preceding clause.
segments.s660-g52: This line contains a sequence of imperatives/commands without an explicit conjunction. ابا is used as an imperative/verb meaning 'be averse to / refuse / shun' and takes دشمن ('the enemy') as its object. دوست پرخاش کن is a second imperative clause: پرخاش کن 'assail / reproach' with دوست ('a friend') as its object. The shortness and juxtaposition are characteristic of classical verse imperatives.
segments.s660-g53: وگر introduces a conditional 'if / but if'. گردی here functions as an archaic verbal form meaning 'you become' (2nd‑person), so it is a conjugated verb rather than the lexical senses given in the list; اندر جهان 'in the world' is a locational phrase, and ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed / honored'. The overall construction is a conditional: 'if you become esteemed in the world'.
segments.s660-g54: ز here means 'about / concerning' or 'from' in a poetic sense and links to the following noun. درد تن is a genitival phrase 'the body's pain' (درد = physical pain; تن = body). اندیش is an imperative 'think / consider' addressed to the addressee. و connects the next phrase, درد گزند = 'the pain/harm of injury' or 'the hurt that comes from harm' (گزند = harm).
segments.s660-g55: سرای is used as the noun 'house/abode' here (the provided lemma senses do not match this occurrence). سپنج is the predicate noun 'sponge' used metaphorically. است is the copula 'is'. هرچون که هست is a concessive/adverbial phrase 'however it is / in whatever way it may be' that qualifies the preceding clause.
segments.s660-g56: بدو اندر = 'in/unto it' (بدو = 'to it / in it' here), forming 'in it' with اندر. ایمن = 'safe / secure'. نشاید نشست is an impersonal negative construction: نشاید 'it is not fitting/allowed' + نشست 'to sit / to settle' (infinitive/verb root) — together 'one ought not sit / one cannot sit safely'.
segments.s660-g81: همان نیز places a demonstrative/emphatic 'the same / likewise' before the command. نیکی ... کن is an imperative clause ('do good'), with the verb کن at the end as expected in Persian imperatives. به اندازه is a prepositional phrase ('to/in measure' = 'in moderation') that qualifies how the goodness should be done.
segments.s660-g82: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the source/agent of the following speech. مرد جهاندیده is a noun + postmodifier ('a worldly‑wise man'); جهاندیده follows and qualifies مرد. بشنو is a second‑person imperative ('hear/heed'), and سخن is its object ('words, speech') placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s660-g83: به دنیا گرای is an imperative instruction: به + گرای (an older/poetic verb form meaning 'turn/incline') telling one to 'incline toward the world.' The conjunction و links a parallel imperative: به دین‌دار چشم = 'cast your eye toward the pious/devout' (دین‌دار = 'the one holding/observing religion'), where چشم is used as 'look/gaze.'
segments.s660-g84: که begins a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'). از دین marks source ('from religion'), and بود is the copula linking to the predicate. مرد را places the experiencer/possessor (the man) with the object‑marker را, so رشک و خشم is predicated as belonging to the man — 'the man's envy and anger are from religion.'
segments.s660-g85: هزینه ... کن is an imperative ('spend / make expenditure'), with هزینه as the noun object and کن the imperative verb. به اندازه گنج is a measure phrase ('to the measure/extent of your treasure' = 'in proportion to your wealth'), where به اندازه gives the standard and گنج specifies it.
segments.s660-g86: This is another imperative with دل (your heart) as the direct object: 'make the heart ...'. از بیشی marks source ('from surplus/excess'), so 'make the heart (into) a treasure from surplus.' گنج بی‌رنج is a noun phrase 'a treasure without toil/pain' modifying what the heart should be, and کن is the imperative 'do/make.'
segments.s660-g87: به کردار شاهان پیشین نگر is an imperative instruction: به introduces the object ('on the conduct'), کردار شاهان پیشین is a noun phrase ('the conduct of former/ancient kings') with پیشین post‑modifying شاهان, and نگر is the imperative verb 'look/regard/consider.'
segments.s660-g88: نباید که باشی is an impersonal/modal construction meaning 'it ought not be that you are' (i.e., 'you should not be'); که introduces the subordinate clause. مگر here means 'except' and restricts the predicate: 'except دادگر' — 'you should be nothing but a just one/ruler.' باشی is the present/subjunctive form 'be (you)'.
segments.s660-g97: نگه کن is an imperative composed of نگه (look, take heed) + کن (do!), so here it means 'take heed' or 'pay attention'. بدین is a classical compound behaving like 'به + این' ('by/with this' or 'to this') and together with نامه ('letter') forms the direct object phrase 'this letter'; پندمند is an adjectival modifier meaning 'admonitory/instructive' that qualifies نامه.
segments.s660-g98: دل is the noun 'heart'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within', placing دل inside سرای ('house, chamber'); سپنجی is an adjective 'spongy' describing سرای. مبند is a negative imperative (from بستن) meaning 'do not close / do not shut', so the clause reads 'do not shut the heart inside a spongy chamber.' Note the negative imperative governs the preceding noun phrase as its object.
segments.s660-g99: به دین من is a prepositional/phrase meaning 'as my duty / by my obligation' (دین here = obligation), placed before the clause for emphasis. تو را (you + object marker را) is the direct object; نیکویی is the desired object 'goodness/well-being'. خواستم is the past-tense, 1st-person singular of 'خواستن' ('I wished/wanted'), so the clause means 'I wished goodness for you.'
segments.s660-g100: به دانش is an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase 'by/with knowledge'. دلت را is دِل + ت (2nd-person enclitic) + object marker را = 'your heart' as the direct object. بیاراستم is past 1st-person singular of بیاراستن ('I adorned/arrayed/arranged'), so the clause says 'I adorned your heart with learning.' Note the possessive suffix ت attached to دل marks the possessor.
segments.s660-g101: به راه خداوند is a prepositional phrase 'by / in the way/path of the Lord (God)', which sets the moral/causal ground for the command. خورشید و ماه ('Sun and Moon') follows and functions as the vocative/agents being addressed; classical Persian often places vocatives/subjects after such prepositional phrases.
segments.s660-g102: ز بن دور کن is the imperative clause: ز ('from') + بن ('root/foundation') + دور ('far') + کن (imperative 'make/do'), literally 'make (it) far from the root.' دیو را دستگاه is the object phrase 'the demon's دستگاه' where را marks it as definite object; دستگاه here means 'court/establishment/host.' Together: 'Drive the demon's court far from its root.'
segments.s660-g103: به‌روز و شب is a time phrase 'by day and night / day and night'. این نامه را پیش دار is an imperative: این نامه را ('this letter' + object marker) + پیش دار (imperative 'keep before [you]' or 'keep in front of you'). Here دار is an imperative 'hold/keep' (classical verb usage), so the whole segment commands to keep the letter constantly before oneself.
segments.s660-g104: خرد را is 'wisdom' with the object marker را. به دل indicates location 'in the heart'. داور خویش is a nominative phrase 'your own judge' (خویش = 'own', here formed from خوی + enclitic ش). دار is again the imperative 'keep/hold', so the clause commands 'keep wisdom in your heart as your own judge' — i.e., let wisdom be the judge within your heart.
segments.s660-g121: کنو is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'now' and sets the temporal frame. لاجرم is an adverb 'necessarily/inevitably' modifying the clause. جود is the noun 'generosity/bounty' serving as the semantic subject, and موجود گشت is a compound predication: موجود (present/existing) + گشت (past of شدن 'to become') meaning 'came into existence / became present.' Word order is topical adverbials first, then the noun subject, and the verbal predicate at the end.
segments.s660-g122: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' شاه جهان is a two-word title 'king of the world' (شاه modifying جهان). The second شاه plus محمود is apposition giving the personal name/title 'Shah Mahmud.' گشت is past tense 'became.' The clause thus reads 'when the king of the world became Shah Mahmud.' The repetition of شاه is a poetic appositive construction.
segments.s660-g123: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' بزم جوید is a verb phrase where بزم (feast/banquet/assembly) is the object of جوید ('seeks/wishes for'); جوید is a present-form often used in hypothetical/poetic conditionals. همی‌گر is an archaic modal particle providing a conditional/would sense (roughly 'would' or 'would then'). نبرد here names the martial action 'battle' (understood as 'take up battle' / 'engage in battle'); the clause literally reads 'If he sought the feast, he would (then) [take] battle.'
segments.s660-g124: This clause states the resulting judgment about the action: جهان‌بخش (a title 'world-bestower') is topicalized and marked by را (object/topic marker). این بود کار کرد literally 'this was the work he did' — این 'this', بود the past copula 'was', and کار کرد 'did the work' (کرد is past of کردن). Together: 'This was the work the world-bestower performed.' The syntax is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (topic marked before the copula and verb).
segments.s660-g125: ابوالقاسم is a proper name (kunya) identifying the king. آن is a demonstrative/adjectival particle 'that' used here to introduce an epithet. شاه پیروز و داد is an epithet string: شاه 'king', پیروز 'victorious', and داد used substantively/adjectivally as 'just' (literally 'justice,' here describing the king). The sequence is appositive: 'Abu al‑Qasim, that victorious and just king.'
segments.s660-g126: زمانه is 'the age/era' (here the world/time personified) as the subject. به دیدار او is a prepositional phrase 'at/to his sight/meeting' (به + دیدار + او). شاد باد is a fixed optative formula: شاد 'happy' and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical wish). The phrase means 'may the age be glad/rejoice at seeing him.' Word order: subject (زمانه) + prepositional phrase + optative predicate.
segments.s660-g89: که here introduces a reason/cause ('because' / 'inasmuch as'). نفرین بود is a simple past copular clause (نفرین 'a curse' + بود 'was'), so literally 'there was a curse'. بهر governs the following noun and means 'for/on account of' (reason or target), so بهر بیداد شاه = 'for the king's injustice' (i.e. 'because of the king's oppression'). Word order is typical classical Persian: cause/phrase + copula at the end.
segments.s660-g90: This is a pair of prohibitive imperatives addressed to 'تو' (you). جز داد مپسند literally places the excluded item ('except justice') before the negative imperative مپسند ('do not approve'), yielding 'approve nothing but justice' (جز governs داد). The coordinate و links a second prohibition نفرین مخواه ('do not desire/ask for a curse'); مخواه is a negative imperative of خواستن. Classical Persian typically puts the verb at the end and allows the excluded noun before the verb.
segments.s660-g91: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. سر و تاج is a tautological pairing (head and crown) used as a compound referring to person and sovereignty; the two nouns are linked by و. شاهنشهان (pl. form of شاهنشاه) names the imperial dignity ('emperor'/'king of kings'). The phrase is verbless (rhetorical question), typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s660-g92: Another rhetorical question introduced by کجا. آن بزرگان ('those great ones') names eminent men; و links the second descriptive phrase فرخ مهان, where فرخ is an epithet meaning 'fortunate/auspicious' modifying مهان ('guests' / nobles in poetic usage). Like the previous segment, the clause is a verbless exclamation asking after vanished worthies.
segments.s660-g93: از ایشان ('of/about them') marks the topic. سخن یادگارست is a nominal clause: سخن 'words/tale' + یادگارست ('is a memento' / 'is memorial'), so 'a tale of them remains as a memorial'. و بس ('and enough') is a short formula meaning 'and that is all' or 'that suffices.' Word order places topic before predicate, as normal in Persian.
segments.s660-g94: This is an SOV clause: 'سرای سپنجی' functions as the noun phrase (literally 'spongy house/dwelling'), نماند is the past negative/preterite 'remained / was left' (here used negatively: 'was not left'), and به کس 'to anyone/no one' completes the idea. Together it means 'the spongy dwelling remained to no one' (i.e. no one now has that dwelling).
segments.s660-g95: This is a nominal/gerundial list describing forbidden acts. گزافه qualifies مفرمانی (excessive/useless disobedience or insubordination), and خون ریختن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase ('shedding blood'). Together they form the objects of the implied prohibition (do not do these things). خون ریختن uses the infinitive ریختن as a noun-like action 'to shed/spill blood.'
segments.s660-g96: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if' or here functions to introduce an alternative forbidden act coordinated with the previous phrase. جنگ را is the definite object (را marks it), and لشکر انگیختن is an infinitive clause 'to rouse/raise the army' (انگیختن = 'to rouse/instigate'). The object جنگ را placed before the verb phrase is typical topical/object-fronting; the whole clause means 'or inciting the army to war.'
segments.s660-g105: اگر introduces a real or potential conditional ('if'). یادگاری کنی is a verbal phrase with the present-subjunctive/imperative form کنی (second singular) after اگر, meaning 'if you make/establish a memorial/inscription.' در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' modifying where the memorial would be made.
segments.s660-g106: که here is a complement/result marker ('so that / that'), linking the conditional clause to its result. نام بزرگی is a genitive phrase 'the name (fame) of greatness.' نگردد is a negative subjunctive/passive-form of گشت (here 'become') meaning 'may not become' or 'will not be made'; نهان is a predicate adjective 'hidden' placed after the verb.
segments.s660-g107: This is an optative/invocational clause: خداوند گیتی ('Lord of the world') is the subject; پناه تو باد uses باد as an optative particle ('may ... be') and means 'may [he] be your refuge/protection.' The word order places the title before the predicate for elevated style.
segments.s660-g108: Another optative clause in parallel: زمان و زمین are coordinated subjects ('time and the earth') and نیک‌خواه تو باد again uses باد as an optative particle meaning 'may [they] be well-wishers / kindly disposed toward you.' Noun + تو is the beneficiary and نیک‌خواه functions as predicate/adjective.
segments.s660-g109: به کام تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/for your desire' (= 'in your favor'). گردنده چرخ بلند is a nominative phrase 'the rotating/high (lofty) wheel' (a poetic image for the heavens/fate), where گردنده (rotating) qualifies چرخ (wheel/heaven) and بلند ('lofty') is an attributive adjective after the noun for emphasis.
segments.s660-g110: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of.' کردار بد is 'evil/bad deeds' (ز + NP = 'away from evil deeds'). The doubled دور و دور is an emphatic repetition meaning 'far and ever farther' or simply 'far, far'; از گزند is another prepositional phrase 'from harm.' Together they wish distance from bad deeds and from harm.
segments.s660-g111: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor.' کاو here functions as an archaic/poetic relative pronoun 'who/that' linking the ruler to the following predicate (note: this specific sense is not present in the supplied sense list). داد دارد خرد is a compact predicate where دارد ('has') governs both داد ('justice') and خرد ('wisdom'), so the whole phrase means 'who has justice and wisdom.'
segments.s660-g112: بکوشد is a third-person-subjunctive/jussive verb meaning 'may he strive / let him strive.' که introduces purpose/result ('that/so that'). با شرم is an instrumental phrase 'with modesty' (شرم here positively as 'modesty'). گرد آورد is a compound verbal expression: گرد (a circumfix/prefixal element here) plus آورد ('bring/produce'), together 'bring about / gather'—the object is supplied by the previous clause (the qualities or their preservation).
segments.s660-g113: This is a sequence of nominal phrases listing qualities that will be linked to the copula 'بود' in the next segment. 'دلیری' (bravery) stands first as a nominal predicate; 'به رزم' uses 'به' to mark the circumstance 'in battle'; 'اندرون زور دست' is a poetic, inverted adjectival phrase meaning 'strength in (his) inner hand/arm' (word order is compressed for verse). There is no finite verb here; these nouns form the subject/predicate that 'بود' will complete.
segments.s660-g114: 'بود' is the past third‑person copula ('he had/was') linking back to the qualities listed before. 'پاکدینی و یزدان‌پرست' are two coordinated nominal/adjectival predicates ('pious' / 'God‑worshipper') joined by 'و'. Together they complete the statement begun in the previous segment.
segments.s660-g115: 'به گیتی نگر' is an imperative phrase: 'look at / consider the world' (به + noun marks the location/circumstance and 'نگر' is the imperative 'look!'). 'کاین هنرها' begins a relative idea ('that these virtues...') and 'کراست' functions as the predicate verb of that relative clause (in the manuscript here it appears as a compact/poetic form). Grammatically the clause links the world‑imperative with the statement that 'these virtues belong to him' (predicate expressed by 'کراست').
segments.s660-g116: 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; 'دیدی' here reads as the temporal 'you see' (common in classical Persian: 'when you see'). The main clause 'ستایش مر او را سزاست' has the noun 'ستایش' (praise) first, followed by the dative 'مر' ('to me'), the direct object 'او را' ('him'), and the predicate 'سزاست' ('it is deserved'). The order emphasizes the noun and the benefactive ('to me') before the judgement 'is deserved'.
segments.s660-g117: 'مجوی' is a negative imperative ('do not seek' — م + جوی). 'آنک' is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the relative clause. 'چون' here means 'like/as', and 'مشتری روشن است' states 'is bright as Jupiter' with 'مشتری' (the planet Jupiter) as the simile and 'روشن' as the predicate adjective; 'است' is the copula. So the whole segment commands: 'Do not seek him who is bright like Jupiter.'
segments.s660-g118: This continues the description of that person: 'جهان‌جوی' is a compound noun 'world‑seeker' (one who seeks worldly gain). The instrumentals 'با تیغ و با جوشن' ('with sword and with armor') enumerate his arms; final 'است' is the copula tying these predicates to the referent of the relative clause. The construction lists character and equipment as attributes of 'the one' warned against above.
segments.s660-g119: This clause is a past transitive statement: 'بستد' is the past third‑person ('he shut/closed'). 'جهان' (the world) appears as the thing closed off (fronted for emphasis), and 'از مردم بت‌پرست' uses 'از' to mark 'from/against' the idol‑worshipping people (i.e., he excluded idolaters). The implied agent is the same subject carried through the surrounding lines (the king/hero).
segments.s660-g120: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'دیبای دین' is a genitive phrase 'the brocade/robe of religion' (a metaphorical image). 'بر دل آیین ببست' literally 'he bound/laid ritual upon the heart': 'بر' = 'upon', 'دل' = 'heart', 'آیین' = 'rite/ceremony', and 'ببست' is the past 'he bound/fastened'. Together the clause says he imposed/affixed religious ritual onto people's hearts, using the metaphor of clothing/tying.
segments.s660-g57: هنر جوی is a verbal-nominal phrase meaning 'seek skill' or 'be a seeker of art/skill' (هنر = skill; جوی = seeker / seek). با دین و دانش گزین is an imperative clause: با governs the instruments 'with', دین و دانش is the coordinated object 'piety (religion) and knowledge', and گزین is the imperative 'choose'. Together it commands: 'seek skill; choose it with religion and learning.'
segments.s660-g58: چو here introduces a conditional 'if'. خواهی is the 2nd‑person present 'you wish'. که begins the subordinate clause. یابی is a subjunctive/optative present meaning 'you obtain / you find' (desire clause: 'if you wish to obtain'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بخت آفرین is a compound meaning 'bounteous/creating fortune' (آفرین used as an adjectival/epithet), i.e. 'by generous fate.'
segments.s660-g59: گرامی کن is an imperative: گرامی 'honored/precious' + کن 'do' → 'honor'. او را marks the definite object 'him'. که introduces the relative clause describing that person. در پیش تو is a locative phrase 'before you / in your presence' (در + پیش 'before/in presence of' + تو 'you').
segments.s660-g60: سپر کرده is a perfect participial construction 'having made/placed as a shield' (سپر = shield; کرده = made/has done). جان بر = '(his) life upon/against' where بر has the sense 'against' here. بداندیش تو is a nominal phrase 'your ill‑wisher' (بداندیش literally 'one who thinks ill' + تو specifying ‘toward you’). The whole phrase means 'who has set his life as a shield against your ill‑wisher.'
segments.s660-g61: به دانش is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with / by means of knowledge'. دو دست ستیزه is a compound object meaning 'the two hands of combat' (دو دست = both hands; ستیزه = fighting/strife), i.e. 'take up both hands for the fight'. ببند is the imperative 'bind / gird on' (here idiomatically 'arm yourself').
segments.s660-g62: چو introduces the conditional 'if'. خواهی 'you wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause. از بد 'from evil' (از = from; بد = evil/bad). نیابی is the negative subjunctive/optative 'may you not find / you may not receive' (negated form 'نه یابی' contracted in poetry) meaning 'do not incur/receive'. گزند 'harm'. So: 'if you wish not to receive harm from evil.'
segments.s660-g63: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. بر سر 'on the head' (بر = on; سر = head). نهی here is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'you put / you place' (poetic present/subjunctive of نهادن); it is not a prohibition in this context. تاج شاهنشهی = 'the crown of kingship' (تاج = crown; شاهنشهی = imperial/kingship).
segments.s660-g64: ره برتری is a nominal phrase 'the way/path of superiority' (ره = way; برتری = superiority). بازجوی here functions as an exhortation 'seek out / investigate' (poetic imperative/verb form) directed back to the subject of the previous clause. از بهی 'through / by means of the good' (از = by/from; بهی = the good/excellent), so: 'seek the path to superiority by what is good.' Note: بازجوی may look like a noun in modern lists, but in this line it is used verbally as 'seek'.
segments.s660-g33: به دانش بود places the preposition به before دانش to express a state 'was in/possessed of knowledge' (literally 'was in knowledge'); بود is the past copula 'was' and شاه زیبای‌تخت is the subject phrase 'the king (of the) beautiful throne' placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s660-g34: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'; داننده is a participial/nominal form 'knower' describing the king; بادی here is a short epithet attached to داننده (poetic/archaic usage rather than its literal 'windy' sense), and و links that epithet to پیروزبخت 'fortunate' as another attribute.
segments.s660-g35: مبادا introduces a negative wish/precaution 'may it not be / lest'; که begins the subordinate clause; گردی here functions as a verb 'you become' (2nd singular subjunctive/optative) rather than the noun 'dust' listed in the lemma data; تو is the 2nd‑person subject pronoun, and پیمان‌شکن is the predicative noun 'oath‑breaker'.
segments.s660-g36: که again introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'; خاک is used as 'dust' here (the corpse‑dust that serves as a shroud), است is the copula 'is', پیمان‌شکن is the possessor noun (marked by را as a definite object/ topical marker), را marks that noun as definite/object, and کفن is 'shroud' — the phrase literally states 'that dust is the shroud of the oath‑breaker.'
segments.s660-g37: به is the preposition 'to/into' marking the target of the verb; باد‌افره is a compound adjective 'wind‑tossed / wind‑blown' describing the place/force to which someone would be thrown; بی‌گناهان is the plural definite/indefinite 'the innocent' (object of the implied action); مکوش is the negative imperative 'do not attempt / do not do' addressed to the listener.
segments.s660-g38: به marks the target of the following verbal idea ('to' or 'toward'); گفتار 'speech' is the noun; بدگوی here modifies گفتار as 'ill‑speaking / slanderous'; مسپار is the imperative 'entrust / give over' (used negatively with the previous clause's sense 'do not...'); گوش is used idiomatically as 'ear' → 'attention/listening' (so 'do not lend an ear to slanderous speech').
segments.s660-g39: به هر کار places the preposition به before 'every affair' to indicate domain ('in every matter'); فرمان مکن is the negative imperative 'do not command'; جز is the preposition 'except'; به‌داد is a prepositional phrase 'for/with respect to justice' (here a nominal use 'except for justice').
segments.s660-g40: که introduces the explanatory/result clause 'so that / because'; از is used here with داد to mean 'from / through (justice)'; داد is the noun 'justice'; باشد is the subjunctive/correlative form of 'to be' ('may be' / 'should be'); روان تو شاد places the possessed noun روان 'soul/spirit' before the adjective شاد 'happy' with the possessive pronoun تو in between — literally 'that from justice may be your soul happy.'
segments.s660-g65: همیشه (adverb) precedes the phrase to stress continual action: “always.” یکی دانشی is an indefinite noun phrase (“one / a certain piece of knowledge”) with یکی (=one/a certain) modifying دانشی (knowledge). پیش دار is an older, compressed verbal expression meaning “keep/have before you” (دار here functions as a directive to hold/have), so the whole segment = “always keep a certain piece of knowledge before you.”
segments.s660-g66: ورا is the object pronoun “it/him/her” referring back to the knowledge; چون functions as a comparative/criterial “as / like / just as”; روان و تن is a paired noun phrase “soul and body”; خوی + ش written split here forms the possessive خویش (“one’s own”), with ش the possessive clitic “his/her/its”; دار repeats the verb “keep/have.” Together: “keep it as (you keep) your soul and body.”
segments.s660-g67: A simple coordination: بزرگان “the great ones / nobles” and بازارگانان “the merchants,” followed by شهر as their locality/attribute (“of the city”). The nouns stand in apposition/qualification: “the nobles and merchants of the city.”
segments.s660-g68: همی modifies داد to mean “always (there should be) justice.” داد is here the noun “justice” and باید is the impersonal/modal “must / it is necessary.” که introduces the purpose/result clause “so that” and یابند is present-subjunctive/optative third-person-plural “they may find/obtain.” بهر is the goal/benefit “advantage.” So: “Always there must be justice so that they may obtain benefit.”
segments.s660-g69: کسی is the indefinite “someone.” کاو is an archaic fused relative (roughly = که او / “who”), introducing the relative clause. ندارد is the present negative “does not have/possess,” and هنر means “skill / art.” با نژاد is a prepositional phrase “by/from lineage / by birth.” So: “someone who does not possess skill by birth.”
segments.s660-g70: مکن is the prohibitive “do not.” زو (classical enclitic) = “of/from him” or “toward him.” به نیز functions together to mean roughly “so/therefore also” or “do not (act so) toward him; instead also …” از کم و بیش is an idiomatic phrase “from little and much” → “whether little or much.” یاد is the noun “remembrance / remembrance of him”; here the sequence urges: “Do not (treat him badly); also remember him whether his merits are small or great.” Grammar: negative imperative + object clitic + adverbial phrase of degree + noun “remembrance.”
segments.s660-g71: مده is the negative imperative “do not give / do not grant.” مرد بی‌نام را is the definite object (“the nameless man” + object marker را). ساز جنگ is a compound meaning “means/instrument of war” (sاز as “device/means” + جنگ “war”), i.e. “weapons / war-equipment” or “to arm for war.” So: “Do not give a nameless man the means of war.”
segments.s660-g72: که introduces the reason/explanation (“for / because”); چون here has a temporal/conditional sense (“when / if”); بازجویی (literally “inquiry/interrogation” in the gloss) functions as “one who seeks / claimant / pursuer” in context; نیاید is “does not come,” به indicates direction/into, and چنگ literally “claw / talon” is used figuratively as “grasp / hand.” The clause says why you should not arm him: when a seeker/avenger comes he will not come into your grasp.
segments.s660-g73: به دشمن is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the enemy' (destination). دهد is a 3rd-person verb 'gives/hands over'. مر is an archaic/clitic element (here not fitting modern senses exactly; it can appear as a possessive/objective clitic in older Persian) and is postposed in older usage; تو را is the explicit 2nd-person object with the object marker را; دوستدار is the noun 'friend/adorer' functioning as the subject after the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). The whole unit reads 'a friend gives/hand(s) you to the enemy.'
segments.s660-g74: دو کار is the noun phrase 'two matters/affairs'. آیدت is the verb 'comes/befalls you' with the enclitic -ت marking 'to you' (3rd-person verb + clitic = 'it comes to you'). پیش here means 'upon/before' in the sense of 'befall', and دشوار و خوار are predicates/adjectives describing those two affairs: 'difficult and despised/abase.'
segments.s660-g75: سلیح تو is 'your weapon' (possessive to follows the noun in classical order). در کارزار is the locative 'into/in the battle'. آورد is a 3rd-person verb 'bring/carry' used here in a jussive/imperative or hortatory sense common in poetry (i.e. 'bring' / 'have brought'). Together: 'Bring your weapon into battle.'
segments.s660-g76: همان 'that same' refers back to the weapon. بر تو is 'on/upon you' (i.e. to your benefit). روزی here functions adverbially ('one day' / 'someday' / 'at a time to come'). به کار آورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'bring into use / be of use'; آورد again is 3rd-person 'will bring/bring about' (future/optative sense in context). Literal: 'That same will one day be brought to your use.'
segments.s660-g77: ببخشای is an archaic imperative/hortative meaning 'give / be generous / grant' (used here as a command). بر مردم مستمند is the prepositional object 'toward needy people' (بر = to/against/toward; مردم مستمند = needy people). The clause instructs: 'Give to needy people / be generous to the needy.'
segments.s660-g78: ز بد دور باش is an imperative phrase: ز (from) + بد (evil/bad) + دور باش 'be far' = 'keep away from evil.' و is 'and'; بترس is an imperative 'fear!/beware!'; از گزند is 'from harm' (از = from, گزند = harm). So: 'Keep away from evil and beware of harm.'
segments.s660-g79: همیشه is the adverb 'always'. نهان is 'hidden/secretly' modifying how the search is done. دل خویش is 'your own heart' (دل = heart; خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'one's own' but here split as خوی + ش in the word list; together mean 'your own'). جوی is an archaic imperative from 'جستن' meaning 'seek/look for'. The clause commands 'always secretly search your own heart.'
segments.s660-g80: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'. رادی and داد are nouns/adjectives here used substantively: رادی 'generosity / magnanimity' and داد 'giving/justice'. هرگز is 'never'. به روی literally 'to the face' = 'openly / publicly'. Together: 'Do not (ever) show generosity and justice openly.'
segments.s660-g1: شنیدم is the past first-person verb 'I heard'. کجا here is an adverbial/introductory particle (literally 'where') used idiomatically to introduce a report ('I heard that...'). کسری شهریار is an appositive noun phrase 'Kisra, the king' (noun + title).
segments.s660-g2: The prepositional phrase به هرمز marks the indirect object 'to/for Hormoz' (به + proper name). یکی نامه is an indefinite object 'a certain letter/a letter' placed before the verb, and کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made/sent' (object before verb is normal in Persian). استوار is an adjective qualifying the letter ('a firm/steadfast letter') placed after the verb phrase; in older/poetic Persian adjectives often follow the noun or phrase they modify.
segments.s660-g3: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of/among' introducing an attributive source (here 'of the kings'). شاه جهاندار is a compound apposition 'king, world-holder/sovereign'. خورشید دهر is a genitival compound 'sun of the age' (خورشید 'sun' followed by دهر 'age' to form 'sun of the age').
segments.s660-g4: مهست is a poetic fused form meaning 'is the moon' or 'is moonlike' (an adjectival/predicative expression with the copula implied). The two و conjunctions link coordinated adjectives/participial phrases: سرافراز 'lofty/exalted' and گیرنده شهر, where گیرنده is a present-participle/agentive 'one who takes/receives' and شهر 'city' is its object (i.e. 'taker/conqueror of the city' or 'holder of the city').
segments.s660-g5: جهاندار (sovereign) is the head noun; بیدار is an adjectival predicate 'awake/alert', and the copula 'is' is implied. The conjunction و links another modifier نیکو کنش where نیکو (good/virtuous) modifies کنش (action/conduct), so together 'of good/virtuous conduct.'
segments.s660-g6: فشاننده is a present-participle/agent noun 'one who scatters/dispenses'; گنج is its direct object 'treasure'. The phrase بی سرزنش is a prepositional-like negative compound 'without reproach' (بی + noun) that qualifies how the treasure is given—i.e. 'dispensing treasure without blame'.
segments.s660-g7: فزاینده is a present-participle 'increasing/augmenting' used attributively. It takes the coordinated objects نام 'name/fame' and تخت قباد 'the throne of Qobad' (تخت followed by the possessor قباد forms the genitive/possessive phrase 'Qobad's throne').
segments.s660-g8: گراینده is another active participle 'one who directs/causes/appoints' functioning as a parallel epithet. It governs the coordinated nouns تاج 'crown', شمشیر 'sword', and داد 'justice'—i.e. 'bestower/arbiter of crown, sword, and justice.' The conjunctions و simply link the list.
segments.s660-g25: بپرسیدم is 1st-person singular past ('I asked'); it comes first and is followed by the prepositional source phrase introduced by از. از marks the person asked ('from/of a well-spoken man'). نیکو‌سخن is a postpositive modifier (compound adjective) meaning 'of good/beautiful speech' (= 'well-spoken').
segments.s660-g26: کسی is an indefinite 'someone'; کاو here functions as an archaic relative ('who' / 'that he') linking back to 'کسی' (this sense is not in the provided list). به distributes over the coordinated nouns 'سال و خرد' and marks the domain ('in years and in wisdom'). بد کهن is an adjectival phrase modifying that someone — an archaic intensifying/age phrase meaning roughly 'very/ancient‑old' (بد here is not used as the ordinary 'bad' in modern sense).
segments.s660-g27: The first که is interrogative 'who'. از ما functions as 'of/among us' in a comparative sense. به یزدان = 'to God' (destination/relationship). The second که introduces the predicate 'نزدیک‌تر' ('nearer') — the clause asks which person is nearer to God.
segments.s660-g28: که‌را is the accusative/interrogative 'whom'. نزد او = 'near/close to him' (نزد = 'in the presence of / close to'). راه باریک‌تر literally 'a narrower/straighter road' — the comparative باریک‌تر modifies راه and completes the question about who has the narrower road to God.
segments.s660-g29: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted subject/verb sequence meaning 'thus gave an answer' (داد = 'gave'). that-clause marker که introduces the reported counsel. دانش گزین is an imperative/hortative instruction 'choose/seek knowledge' with گزین in the sense 'choose/select'.
segments.s660-g30: چو is a temporal/conditional particle 'when/if'; خواهی is 2nd-person present ('you wish' / 'you want'). ز marks source 'from', and پروردگار آفرین is a poetic name for the Creator — آفرین here functions as a divine epithet (present‑stem/agentive sense implying 'the Creator'). The whole phrase means 'whenever you wish, (take it) from the Lord‑Creator.'
segments.s660-g31: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). نادان is the subject 'the ignorant person'. فزونی ندارد is a negated predicate: فزونی 'advantage/excess' + ندارد 'does not have' = 'has no advantage/greater status'. ز خاک 'over dust/from dust' expresses the thing compared (i.e., over the earth/dust).
segments.s660-g32: به دانش means 'with/by knowledge' or 'in regard to knowledge'. بسنده کند is a periphrastic subjunctive/hortative: بسنده (sufficiency/enough) + کند ('let it do' / subjunctive auxiliary) = 'let (it/the soul) be satisfied/suffice'. جان پاک is the noun phrase 'pure soul' (the one that should be content). The clause urges that the pure soul be content with knowledge.
segments.s660-g17: نهادیم is the past tense 1st-person plural 'we placed/set'. The rest gives the localization and object: بر سر تو is the prepositional phrase 'on your head' and تاج زر is the direct object 'a crown of gold'. The clitic را follows تو (an emphatic/definite marking common in older/poetic Persian) while the verb appears initially for stylistic emphasis.
segments.s660-g18: چنان هم که ... introduces a comparative/relational clause 'just as' (چنان هم که = 'just as/so too that'). ما یافتیم is past 1pl 'we found/received', and از پدر gives the source 'from (our) father.' The clause explains that the crown was placed in the same manner as it had been received from the father.
segments.s660-g19: همان آفرین is 'that very praise/acclamation' (همان = 'the same/that very', آفرین = 'praise/acclamation'). نیز 'also/likewise' adds parallelism; کردیم is past 1pl 'we made/did', and یاد here is the noun 'remembrance/commemoration' forming the compound verbal idea 'یاد کردن' (to commemorate), rendered here as 'we commemorated'. The verb and noun are inverted for poetic effect (کردیم یاد instead of یاد کردیم).
segments.s660-g20: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر تاج ما is the locative 'upon our crown', کرد is past 3rd-singular 'he placed/did', and فرخ قباد is the agent's name; in Persian poetry the subject often follows the verb. The clause therefore means 'which Farakh Qobad put on our crown.'
segments.s660-g21: This is imperative address to 'you' (تو). بیدار باش and جهاندار باش are two coordinated imperatives: 'be awake/alert' and 'be a ruler/sovereign'. The repeated باش is the imperative 'be', giving a rhythmic instruction.
segments.s660-g22: A series of adjectives joined by و culminates in the imperative باش: 'be wise (خردمند) and generous (راد) and harmless (بی‌آزار)'. The single final باش applies to the whole coordinated list.
segments.s660-g23: This line uses two parallel imperatives introduced by the preposition به: به دانش فزای = 'increase in knowledge' (فزای is an imperative/optative form telling the addressee to increase) and به یزدان گرای = 'turn/lean toward God' (گرای functions here as an imperative 'incline/turn to'). The two imperatives are joined by و for a balanced exhortation.
segments.s660-g24: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause 'for/that'. اوی‌ست is an archaic contracted copula 'he/it is' (او + است). جان تو را is 'your soul' with تو marked by را for definiteness/emphasis, and رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide' — 'for He is the guide of your soul.'
segments.s660-g9: که is the relative particle 'who/which' introducing a clause describing the person; با governs a list of nouns ('with ...') and links them as instruments/qualities. فر here is the technical poetic noun 'royal glory', برزست is an adjective 'exalted' paired with فر, then فرهنگ and نام continue the coordinated list (connected by و), meaning 'learning/culture and fame'. Word order is noun + coordinating conjunctions typical of Persian poetic listing.
segments.s660-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by'; تاج بزرگی is a genitival phrase 'a crown of greatness' (noun + ezāfe omitted in verse), and رسیده is a past participle meaning 'has reached/attained' (used predicatively of the same implied subject). به کام is a fixed idiom 'to/into the wish; to fulfillment' where کام means 'satisfaction/fulfillment'. The clause expresses result: 'has attained to the satisfaction of a great crown.'
segments.s660-g11: سوی is a preposition 'toward / in the direction of' (here giving the recipient/occasion). پاک is an adjective 'pure' modifying هرمزد; هرمزد is a personal name here (followed by apposition فرزند ما 'our son'), so the whole chunk marks the person to whom/for whom the action is directed. Word order is preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s660-g12: پذیرفته is a past participle 'accepted/received' serving as the main verbal idea (perfect meaning 'has accepted'). از دل is an adverbial phrase 'from the heart' indicating sincerity. همی is an archaic/enclitic intensifier meaning roughly 'indeed/truly' here; پند ما is the object 'our counsel'. The sequence is typical classical Persian: participle + adverbials + object.
segments.s660-g13: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from/by the gods/God' marking source or agent of the blessing. بدی is an archaic jussive/optative form meaning roughly 'may (he) be given' or 'grant (to him)' (not the modern noun 'evil'); it introduces the wish. شاد و پیروز are adjectives 'joyful and victorious' that qualify بخت 'fortune' (بخت is the noun being described). Grammatically this is an optative blessing: 'From God may (his) fortune be joyful and victorious.'
segments.s660-g14: همیشه is the adverb 'always/ever'; جهاندار is a nominal predicate 'world‑ruler/sovereign'. با + noun phrase 'with crown and throne' supplies accompaniment/possession. The whole segment continues the blessing: 'may he always be sovereign with crown and throne.' Word order places the temporal adverb first, then the predicate, then the comitative phrase.
segments.s660-g15: به is the preposition 'in/on/at' marking temporal circumstance; ماه خجسته is 'the blessed/auspicious month' (noun + adjective), and به خرداد روز specifies the exact day 'on the day of Khordad' (Khordad is the month-name). Repetition of به is a poetic device to list time phrases individually.
segments.s660-g16: به again marks circumstance 'with' or 'under'; نیک اختر is a compound 'favorable/auspicious star' and فال is 'omen' coordinated with it. گیتی‌فروز is a poetic adjective 'world‑illuminating' modifying فال. The phrase gives the auspicious signs connected to the time: 'with a good star and a world‑illuminating omen.'
segments.s660-g41: زبان را is the direct object (زبان 'speech/utterance' + را object marker). مگردان is a negative imperative (do not turn/turn away). به‌گرد دروغ is a prepositional phrase 'toward/about falsehood' (poetic spacing), so the verb governs that phrase as its directional complement.
segments.s660-g42: چو خواهی begins a conditional clause ('if you want/wish'); که is the complementizer linking خواهی to the subordinate clause. تختِ تو is the subject/semantic theme ('your throne'), گیرد is a third-person verb form ('takes/receives' — here optative/desired sense in the conditional), and فروغ is the thing gained ('radiance/splendour') placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s660-g43: وگر is a conditional 'if'; زیردستی is the nominative/subject 'an underling' (poetic noun juxtaposition indicates role); بود is the copula ('was/was there' — here introducing the hypothetical existence/state) and گنج‌دار is the predicate noun 'treasurer/treasure-holder'. The clause sets the condition for the following instruction.
segments.s660-g44: تو (explicit subject 'you') precedes the object phrase او را ('him' with object marker). ازان گنج is a genitive/source phrase 'of that treasure' (from that treasure). بی‌رنج دار is an imperative/commandal phrase 'keep/give (it) without trouble' — here دار functions as a verbal command in archaic/poetic usage (the supplied noun senses do not include this verbal use).
segments.s660-g45: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). چیزِ کسان is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the belongings/things of the people' (classical juxtaposition). دشمنِ گنج is a predicate meaning 'an enemy to treasure' and تست is an archaic poetic form corresponding to 'is/it is to you' (a contraction/function like 'است' tied to the second person) — that verbal sense is not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s660-g46: بدان here functions as به + آن 'to/for that' (poetic contraction: 'to that [treasure]'). گنج is the referent. شو is the imperative 'become / be' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad'. کز introduces the causal clause 'because/from which' and رنج تست gives the reason 'it is from (your) toil/suffering' — تست is the archaic copular/possessive form not represented in the supplied senses.
segments.s660-g47: وگر sets the conditional 'if'; زیردستی is the subject 'an underling'; شود is the verb 'becomes' (present/subjunctive sense 'becomes/turns into'); and مایه‌دار is the predicate adjective 'wealthy/rich'. The clause mirrors the prior conditional construction with a different outcome.
segments.s660-g48: همان is emphatic 'that very [one]'; شهریارش is 'his king' (شهریار + possessive -ش in poetic form). بود is the copula 'is/will be' linking to the predicate, and سایه‌دار is a compound noun 'shadow-holder' used idiomatically for an influential backer/protector. The sentence therefore says the newly rich underling becomes a source of support for the king.

sentences.s660-s25: One should remain in your protection — whether he is a subordinate or a court‑attendant.
sentences.s660-s26: When someone does you a kindness, reward him; be averse to the enemy, and assail (verbal‑attack) a friend.
sentences.s660-s27: But if you become esteemed in the world, consider the body's pain and the hurt of harm.
sentences.s660-s28: The house is a sponge; however it may be, one should not sit there in safety.
sentences.s660-s41: Do that same good in moderation; listen to the words of a worldly‑wise man.
sentences.s660-s42: Turn toward the world and fix your gaze on the devout, for a man's envy and anger come from religion.
sentences.s660-s43: Spend in proportion to your treasure; make your heart a treasure, free from excess and trouble.
sentences.s660-s44: Consider the conduct of former kings; you should be nothing except just.
sentences.s660-s49: Heed this admonitory letter; do not shut your heart up in a spongy chamber.
sentences.s660-s50: As my duty I wished good for you; with learning I adorned your heart.
sentences.s660-s51: O Sun and Moon, by the way of the Lord, drive the demon's court far from its root.
sentences.s660-s52: Keep this letter before you day and night; keep wisdom in your heart as your own judge.
sentences.s660-s61: Now, inevitably, generosity came into being when the king of the world became Shah Mahmud.
sentences.s660-s62: If he sought the feast, he would take up battle; this was the work the world-bestower performed.
sentences.s660-s63: Abu al‑Qasim, that victorious and just king — may the age rejoice at his sight.
sentences.s660-s45: Because a curse attends a king's oppression, accept nothing except justice, and ask not for a curse.
sentences.s660-s46: Where is that head and crown of the emperors? Where are those great men and fortunate nobles?
sentences.s660-s47: Of them, speech remains as a memorial and that is enough; the spongy dwelling was left to no one.
sentences.s660-s48: Do not, without cause, commit insubordination and shed blood, nor incite an army to war.
sentences.s660-s53: If you make a memorial in the world so that the great name will not be hidden.
sentences.s660-s54: May the Lord of the world be your refuge; may time and the earth be kindly disposed toward you.
sentences.s660-s55: May the lofty, revolving wheel turn to your desire; far from evil deeds and far from harm.
sentences.s660-s56: May the king who possesses justice and wisdom strive to bring it about with modesty.
sentences.s660-s57: He possessed bravery in battle and strength in his hand; he was devout and God‑worshipping.
sentences.s660-s58: Look upon the world — these virtues are his; when you see him, praise him, for he deserves it from me.
sentences.s660-s59: Do not seek the one who is bright as the planet Jupiter; he seeks the world and is armed with sword and armor.
sentences.s660-s60: He shut the world against idolaters; from religion's splendid robe he bound ritual upon the heart.
sentences.s660-s29: Seek skill; choose (it) with piety and learning if you wish to attain it by generous fortune.
sentences.s660-s30: Honor him who, before you, has placed his life as a shield against your ill‑wisher.
sentences.s660-s31: With learning, gird yourself with both hands for battle if you would avoid suffering harm from evil.
sentences.s660-s32: When you place the crown of kingship upon your head, seek out the path to superiority through what is good.
sentences.s660-s17: The king of the fair throne was learned; he was a knower, wind‑borne (an epithet) and fortunate.
sentences.s660-s18: Lest you become an oath‑breaker; for the oath‑breaker’s shroud is dust.
sentences.s660-s19: Do not cast the innocent to the wind‑tossed (i.e. do not expose the innocent to the winds); do not lend an ear to slanderous speech.
sentences.s660-s20: Do not issue commands in every affair, except in justice; so that, by justice, your soul may be glad.
sentences.s660-s33: Always keep a certain piece of knowledge before you; keep it as your soul and body.
sentences.s660-s34: The great men and the merchants of the city — justice must always be given so that they may obtain benefit.
sentences.s660-s35: Do not (dismiss) anyone who has not acquired skill by birth; do not treat him thus, but remember him whether his merit is little or great.
sentences.s660-s36: Do not arm (or equip) a nameless man for war, for when a seeker/claimant comes he will not fall into your grasp.
sentences.s660-s37: If a friend hands you over to the enemy, two things will befall you: hardship and disgrace.
sentences.s660-s38: Bring your weapon into the battlefield; that same weapon will one day be of use to you.
sentences.s660-s39: Be generous to needy people; keep far from evil and beware of harm.
sentences.s660-s40: Always search your heart in secret; never display generosity or justice openly.
sentences.s660-s1: I heard that Khosrow the king once sent a firm letter to Hormoz.
sentences.s660-s2: He is, among kings, the world-ruler and the sun of the age; moonlike, exalted, and taker of the city.
sentences.s660-s3: The sovereign is alert and of good conduct, a dispenser of treasure without reproach.
sentences.s660-s4: Increasing renown and Qobad's throne, bestower of crown, sword, and justice.
sentences.s660-s13: I asked a well-spoken man — someone who was advanced in years and age-old in wisdom.
sentences.s660-s14: Who among us is nearer to God? To whom is the way to Him narrower?
sentences.s660-s15: He answered thus: 'Choose knowledge — whenever you wish, take it from the Lord‑Creator.'
sentences.s660-s16: For the ignorant has no superiority over dust; let the pure soul be content with knowledge.
sentences.s660-s9: We set a golden crown upon your head, just as we had received from our father.
sentences.s660-s10: We likewise made a remembrance of that very praise which Farakh Qobad placed on our crown.
sentences.s660-s11: Be watchful and be a sovereign; be wise, generous, and harmless.
sentences.s660-s12: Increase your learning and turn to God, for He is the guide of your soul.
sentences.s660-s5: who, by royal glory and exalted eminence, with learning and renown, has attained the fulfillment of a great crown.
sentences.s660-s6: He has accepted our counsel from the heart toward the pure Hormozd, our son.
sentences.s660-s7: May his fortune, from the gods, be joyful and victorious; may he ever be sovereign with crown and throne.
sentences.s660-s8: On the blessed month, on the day of Khordad, with a favorable star and a world‑illuminating omen.
sentences.s660-s21: Do not turn your tongue toward falsehood, if you wish your throne to take on radiance.
sentences.s660-s22: And if an underling is a treasurer, you should give him of that treasure without trouble.
sentences.s660-s23: For the property of the people is an enemy to treasure; be glad for that treasure, for it comes from your toil.
sentences.s660-s24: And if an underling becomes wealthy, that very man will be his king's influential backer.
